retrieved_docs.title: Angular Chelitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Angular cheilitis (AC) is a highly manageable condition. AC is mostly curable and poses no inherent risk to life, and rarely results in permanent disfigurement. AC improves within the first several days of successful treatment and typically resolves by 2 weeks; thus, schedule a follow-up then. Chronic cases can provoke atrophy or granulation formation at the angles of the mouth. In one study done over 5 years, AC had a recurrence rate of 80%. Identification and management of underlying risk factors is a necessity to prevent recurrence. When non-modifiable risk factors exist, when modifiable risk factors go unaddressed, or when the treatment course is incomplete, repeat bouts of AC are commonplace. Common reasons for recurrence are failure to identify and treat oral candidiasis and continued poor oral and denture hygiene. If relapses are frequent, prolong treatment past 2 weeks and use preventive measures with topical emollients or antifungals.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Complications -- Inhalation or Ingestion of Crown
retrieved_docs.content: A rubber dam is to be placed till cementation to prevent aspiration of the crown. Sometimes, the rubber dam may be removed prior to preparing the distal surface. Even after all the measures taken, accidents do happen. Clinicians and their staff must remain calm to make timely decisions to avoid potentially dangerous complications and save their patients' lives. The dentist's responsibility is to be alert to both early and delayed signs and symptoms of an aspiration accident and respond quickly if and when it occurs. High vacuum suctioning, patting on the back after placing the patient in the prone position, Heimlich maneuver should be tried to yield the crown. A clinical referral for a rapid chest radiograph should be made if still unsuccessful in retrieving the crown. The crown can be removed via bronchoscopy if it remains in the lung or bronchi. Immediate medical and surgical intervention would be required. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The most common postoperative complication is incision line opening. The design of the removable interim prosthesis is involved, it is corrected. The patient is instructed to rinse 2-3 times daily with chlorhexidine. If granulation process extends for more than two weeks, epithelial margin trimming can be done. If implants become exposed during the healing period, no attempt should be made to cover them with tissue. Rather denture is relieved aggressively over the area with implant exposure. The mobility of the implant during healing is unusual but may occur, mostly accompanied by a radiolucent zone around the implant. Whatever may be the cause, the implant should be removed. Signs and symptoms of failure for an implant are horizontal mobility  greater than 0.5 mm, rapid progressive bone loss, pain during percussion, uncontrolled exudate, generalized radiolucency around the implant, more than one half of the bone is lost around the implant and last the implants inserted in poor position, making them useless for prosthetic support. A success rate of 85%at the end of 5 year period and 80% at the end of 10 year period are minimum criteria for success. [15] [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment-Related Complications Chemotherapy Side Effects When utilizing chemotherapy, the majority of the side effects occur during the treatment process. Nausea, malaise, alopecia, anemia, and anorexia are possible but usually resolve shortly after the completion of the chemotherapy cycle. There are, however, some long-term side effects, which may include cardiotoxicity, pulmonary toxicity, and gradual hearing loss. There are reports of later development of a secondary malignancy. [19] Radiation Side Effects Radiation is known to impart superficial side effects, including skin dryness, itching, peeling, and uncommonly, burns. Menstrual changes, erectile dysfunction, and infertility are all reported adverse events in cases of pelvic radiation. When the chest and abdomen are involved in radiation treatment, diarrhea, incontinence, rectal bleeding, nausea, vomiting, dry mouth, dysphagia, pneumonitis, and fibrosis are possible. Much like chemotherapy, there is a small risk of late development of a secondary malignancy. [20] Periprosthetic Infection Prostheses-related infections are a relatively frequent complication (approximately 10% of limb salvage surgeries) most often due to lengthy surgery time, repeated surgery at the same site, and immunosuppression secondary to chemotherapy. First-line treatment of these periprosthetic infections typically involves one or more debridement procedures with both local and systemic antibiotic therapy (systemic and local antibiotic cement beads). If these efforts are ineffective, the implant requires removal, followed by debridement and washout. A cement spacer impregnated with an antibiotic generally gets placed before the insertion of a new prosthesis. Ultimately, amputation may be necessary for a number of these patients. [11] Implant Failure The most common reason for reconstruction failure is the mechanical breakdown of the mega prosthesis. Mechanical failure necessitates the replacement of the prosthetic. The tibia is the most frequent site of mechanical failure. [11] Fracture/Non-Union of Allograft/Autograft Fracture/non-union of allograft/autograft reconstruction is a relatively infrequent complication, but it does occur. Chemotherapy, radiation, and extracorporeal treatment of autograft bone have been reported to increase the risk of these complications. Refractory cases may necessitate metallic implant placement or amputation. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Prosthetics in Orthopedics -- Issues of Concern -- Complications in Prosthetic Care
retrieved_docs.content: Prosthesis usage is not without associated complications. One of the most notable complications is that of choke syndrome.  This syndrome is characterized by venous outflow obstruction due to a socket that is too snug and is more common in lower extremity prostheses. In the acute phase, the skin over the stump site becomes red and indurated and may result in an "orange-peel" appearance. The chronic phase may result if the constriction is not resolved and may result in hemosiderin deposits and venous stasis ulcers.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Management of Patients Undergoing Radiation Therapy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Performing pre-radiation dental extractions is a controversial topic since the evidence available is contradictory. Some studies have shown that pre-radiation extractions may increase the risk of osteoradionecrosis, and others show that the lack of pre-radiation extractions increases this risk too. If extractions are performed before the first cycle of radiation therapy, the ideal interval between extraction and the beginning of radiation therapy should be between 10 days and 3 weeks so that complete soft tissue healing occurs. [2] The urgency to start radiation therapy is a critical determining factor in whether to extract teeth or not. [20] Radiation treatment should not be unnecessarily delayed as it diminishes a patient's survival. Wound dehiscence could be a limiting factor in delaying therapy at times. Daily wound dressing, irrigation, and antibiotic cover may hasten the process. Finally, obtaining dental impressions pre-radiation therapy for study models that are useful for fabricating soft mouth guards or medicament-carrying trays is recommended. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Stability in Mandibular Denture -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: An unstable denture may result in reduced maximum bite forces. Due to disuse atrophy, the jaw muscles may weaken in such cases. [9] This situation is a vicious cycle as the resultant weakening of the jaw muscles may further reduce maximum bite forces during mastication. This effect is more pronounced in patients with long-term use of conventional dentures and unstable lower dentures. [10] An unstable denture will affect not only the masticatory efficiency but also the psychology of the patient. In patients, particularly those who are more active socially, unstable dentures may lead to social isolation due to embarrassment; this also affects the patient's diet and leads to malnutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: Periprosthetic Proximal Femur Fractures -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Before the radiographic evaluation, the differential diagnosis should include: Periprosthetic fracture Aseptic loosening Prosthetic joint infection Ligamentous injury Fracture of the metal or polyethylene implant Contusion Hip dislocation Venous thromboembolism Occult fracture Metallosis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 310
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late effect of fracture of the spine and/or trunk without spinal cord lesion, the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية (late effects of fracture of the lower extremities), the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية (late effects of fracture of the upper extremities), and the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all described as being associated with كسر (fracture). This indicates that delayed complications can arise after fractures affecting various skeletal regions, including multiple bones, the spine or trunk, the lower and upper limbs, and the femoral neck, and that these sequelae are intrinsically linked to the initial fracture event.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2602
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, First use syndrome of dialysis, Sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are medical conditions that share a common clinical characteristic: a sudden‑onset course. In each of these disorders the disease manifests abruptly, indicating that their clinical course is defined by a rapid appearance of symptoms. Recognizing this sudden‑onset pattern is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of complications related to hemodialysis, initial dialysis use, retinal degeneration, or dialysis dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network unifies head and neck trauma, intracranial and skull‑base injuries, all skeletal fractures, their associated tissue damage, poisoning mechanisms, external causes (MVT, aircraft crash), procedural treatments, and the spectrum of late‑effect sequelae under the central concepts of fracture and late effects of unspecified injury, providing a comprehensive framework for injury classification, management, and long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2143
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis and First use syndrome of dialysis are both dialysis‑related conditions that share a Sudden onset (contextual qualifier) (qualifier value) as their clinical course. The Sudden onset (contextual qualifier) (qualifier value) itself is identified as the clinical course of متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome). Together, these relationships emphasize that abrupt presentation is a defining feature of several hemodialysis complications, underscoring the need for rapid recognition and intervention in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 620

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1961
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis is linked to a spectrum of acute and chronic complications. Acute disorder of hemodialysis and First use syndrome of dialysis both arise as direct consequences of hemodialysis and are characterized by a sudden‑onset clinical course. The dialysis‑related condition described as متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis‑syndrome) is also caused by hemodialysis and shares this abrupt clinical presentation. Clinically, hemodialysis is associated with chronic hypertension, اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal functional impairment), فقر دم صغير الكريات (microcytic anemia), and acute renal failure (قصور كلوي حاد). Hemodialysis can also precipitate low blood pressure (ضغط الدم المنخفض). In addition, Sudden acquired retinal degeneration is noted to have a sudden‑onset clinical course, illustrating that abrupt onset is a common qualifier for several severe medical events. These interrelationships underscore the importance of vigilant monitoring for both systemic and organ‑specific adverse effects in patients receiving hemodialysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: relationships form a structured network in which fractures—whether of multiple bones, the spine, trunk, limbs, femoral neck, or skull vault—are intricately linked to specific late‑effect conditions, anatomical locations, treatment morphologies, broader injury classifications, and associated behavioral factors, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding fracture sequelae and related injuries in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that these types of injuries are frequently described as occurring because of specific causative events. This association highlights the importance of identifying the underlying mechanisms when evaluating patients with crushing or internal injuries, as the wording “due to” often signals a direct link between the injury and its precipitating factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2169
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are all described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset. The “sudden onset” qualifier indicates that the manifestations of these conditions develop rapidly, which is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when diagnosing and managing these disorders. Understanding that a rapid, abrupt presentation is common to these entities helps guide timely evaluation and appropriate therapeutic interventions.
